1: levena (lĕbēnâ): 'brick/tile' — unbaked clay tablets were the writing material of Mesopotamia (cuneiform tablets); here Ezekiel inscribes (chuqita, ḥuqîtā) Jerusalem onto the clay, turning the Babylonian medium of record-keeping into the visual surface for Israel's judgment. The sign-act (ôt) transforms the prophet's body and surroundings into a prophetic performance — enacted oracle rather than spoken oracle. The use of a clay tile is culturally resonant: Babylon conquered by its own medium.
2: The siege works: dayeq (dāyeq, siege wall), solelah (sōlĕlâ, ramp/embankment), machanot (maḥănôt, encampments), karim (kārîm, battering rams). All four elements of ancient siege warfare arrayed against the clay Jerusalem. The enumeration is technical — Ezekiel has seen siege warfare in his lifetime and describes the full complement of ancient military siege engineering. The sign-act forces the exiles to watch their city being besieged in miniature, months before the historical siege will be complete.
3: machavat barzel (maḥăbat barzel): 'iron griddle/pan' — placed as an iron wall (qir barzel, qîr barzel) between Ezekiel and the clay city. The iron wall between prophet and Jerusalem represents the divine separation — YHWH has erected an iron barrier between himself and the city, shutting off the normal channels of prophetic intercession. The sign concludes: zeh ot (zeh ʾôt), 'this is a sign to the house of Israel.' The sign is not merely illustrative but constitutive — it participates in the reality it depicts.
4: nasa avon (nāśāʾ ʿāwôn): 'to bear/lift up iniquity.' This is one of the most important theological terms in the OT — the verb nāśāʾ (to carry, lift, bear) with its object ʿāwôn (iniquity, guilt, punishment). Ezekiel lying on his side bearing Israel's iniquity is a prophetic sign-act of substitutionary bearing: the prophet physically enacts the weight of covenant guilt through his constrained body. The same verbal combination (nāśāʾ ʿāwôn) appears in Isaiah 53:11-12 (the Servant bears the iniquity of many) and Numbers 14:34 (you shall bear your iniquity forty years). The 390-day performance is the physical vocabulary of covenant guilt-bearing.
5: Three hundred and ninety days (shalosh maot vetishim yom) = the years of Israel's iniquity. The number 390 has been variously calculated: as the sum of years from the division of the kingdom (931 BCE) to the end of exile; as the period from the Exodus to the temple dedication minus the 40 wilderness years; or as a typological number rather than a precise historical calculation. The key theological move is the day-for-year principle (yom lashanah yom lashanah, yôm lāšānâ): Ezekiel's physical ordeal compresses centuries of covenant history into months of bodily suffering.
6: Forty days for Judah's iniquity — a day for each year. The 40-year connection to the wilderness wandering (Numbers 14:34 explicitly uses the same day-for-year equation) casts the Babylonian exile as a second wilderness: a period of purging and preparation for a second entry into the land. The temporal symbolism is not precise chronology but covenantal typology.
7: 'Turn your face toward the besieged Jerusalem with your arm bared' — zeroa chasufah (zĕrôaʿ ḥăśûp̄â): 'bared arm,' the idiom for divine power in action (Isaiah 52:10: YHWH bares his holy arm before all the nations; Ezekiel 53:1: to whom has the arm of YHWH been revealed?). The prophet's bared arm facing the clay Jerusalem embodies YHWH's own bared-arm judgment, being executed through the Babylonian siege. The prophet's body is the divine action made visible.
8: 'I will put cords on you so that you cannot turn from side to side until you have completed the days of your siege.' The immobilization that was announced in 3:25 is now specified as part of the sign-act: the physical constraint on Ezekiel's body enacts the constraint of the siege on Jerusalem. The immobility is simultaneously the prophet's sign-act and his personal bearing of the city's judgment.
9: The mixed-grain bread: chita useorah uful ve'adashim udochan vekhussemeth — wheat, barley, beans, lentils, millet, and emmer wheat. This is siege-ration food — the mixture of available grains that a population under siege would bake when normal supplies were exhausted. The bread of distress (cf. Isaiah 30:20 'bread of distress and water of affliction') is the physical diet of the sign-act period, connecting the prophet's 390-day bodily experience to the lived experience of a besieged city.
10: Twenty shekels of food per day (esrim shekel, ʿeśrîm šeqel) — approximately 8 oz. / 225 grams, a starvation ration. The measured weight and set intervals (meit ad eit tochalennu, mēʿēt ʿad ʿēt tôʾkĕlennû) impose the discipline of famine proportioning on the prophet. The careful measurement dramatizes the systematic exhaustion of food supplies in a siege.
11: Water rationed at one-sixth of a hin (šiššît hahîn) — approximately 0.5 liters per day, severely inadequate for normal functioning. The combined food and water rations picture total deprivation: a person on these quantities would be visibly weakening, which is the visible sign. The prophet's deteriorating body is the living prophecy of Jerusalem's deterioration under siege.
12: Baked over gallelei tseat ha-adam (gĕlālê ṣēʾat hāʾādām): 'discs of human excrement.' The ultimate ritual defilement for a priest: cooking over human waste. The orderly, detail-oriented priest who has spent his life maintaining purity distinctions is commanded to violate the deepest levels of priestly cleanness. This enacts the defilement of the exile itself — Israel driven among the nations will eat defiled (tameh) bread, unable to maintain ritual purity in diaspora conditions.
13: 'This is how the Israelites will eat their defiled bread among the nations to which I will scatter them.' The sign-act explains its own meaning: the prophet's defilement in the sign = the nation's defilement in exile. The verb adam (ʾādām) in context: ṭāmēʾ (defiled/unclean) is the technical priestly term (Leviticus 11:43) — what Ezekiel enacts in the sign, the exiles will live in the nations where maintaining ritual purity is impossible.
14: Ezekiel protests (achah Adonai YHWH, ʾăhāh ʾădōnāy Yhwh): 'Ah, Lord GOD!' — the formula of prophetic protest (Jeremiah 1:6; Ezekiel 4:10; Ezekiel 14:13). His protest centers on his priestly cleanness: from youth to the present (mineuray ve'ad atah) he has never eaten what died naturally or was mauled — never eaten nevelah (carrion) or terefa (torn flesh), both prohibited in Lev 11 and 17. The protest is not disobedience but priestly integrity — the covenant-keeper for whom defilement is not just distasteful but self-defining.
15: Divine concession: cattle dung (tsapat habaqar, ṣĕpieʿ habbāqār) instead of human dung. The divine accommodation to the prophet's priestly integrity allows a less ritually defiling fuel (cattle dung was commonly used as fuel in the ancient Near East). The concession does not annul the sign but modifies its medium — the defilement is still present (impure fuel for a priest) but not at the maximum level. God's responsiveness to the prophet's conscientious objection is itself a theological datum.
16: 'I will break the staff of bread (mateh lechem, maṭṭēh leḥem) in Jerusalem.' The staff of bread — bread as the support/staff that sustains life (Leviticus 26:26; Psalms 105:16; Isaiah 3:1) — will be broken: the food supply will be cut off. The inhabitants will eat their rationed bread with dread (deagah, dĕʾāgâ) and drink their measured water in horror (shamemah, šāmĕmâ). The emotional states (dread, horror) as well as the physical deprivation will mark the siege-life of Jerusalem.
17: 'They will lack bread and water, look at each other in horror (nashammu ish veachiv, nāšammû ʾîš wĕʾāḥîw: they will be desolate, each man with his brother), and waste away (nemaqu, nāmaqû) because of their iniquity.' The final outcome: communal horror and individual wasting. Māqaq (to rot, waste away) is used for physical decay — the iniquity that caused the siege now causes the physical deterioration of the besieged. The connection between moral state and physical condition is explicit: they waste away because of their iniquity, not merely because of food shortage.
